examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency list: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[]. Step 2: Start DFS from node 0: visited={0}, check neighbor 1, DFS from 1: visited={0,1}, no neighbors, return false. No cycle found from node 0. Step 3: Start DFS from node 1: visited={1}, no neighbors, return false. No cycle found from node 1. Step 4: All nodes processed without cycle, return true.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0]. Step 2: Start DFS from node 0: visited={0}, check neighbor 1, DFS from 1: visited={0,1}, check neighbor 0, node 0 is in visited, cycle detected, return true. Step 3: Since DFS from node 0 found cycle, return false immediately.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS from each node to detect cycles by exploring all possible paths, checking if any node is revisited in the same traversal. Use it for small graphs or as a baseline, but it's inefficient due to redundant checks.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Course Schedule Feasibility with Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution to detect cycles in a directed graph using brute force DFS from each node.

explanation.prose: This is the complete problem solution using the brute force DFS O(n²) approach. The intuition is that a course schedule is feasible if there are no cycles in the directed graph where nodes represent courses and edges represent prerequisites. If a cycle exists, it creates a circular dependency making completion impossible. The brute force approach works by performing DFS from each node to check if any node can reach itself through a path, indicating a cycle. We start DFS from each node with a fresh visited set for each traversal to avoid interference between different starting points. During each DFS, if we encounter a node already in the current path, we've found a cycle. The algorithm: 1) Build adjacency list from prerequisites. 2) For each node from 0 to n-1: a) Initialize empty set for current path. b) Perform DFS from current node. c) If DFS returns true (cycle found), return false (schedule impossible). 3) If all nodes processed without finding cycle, return true.

connectsTo.reason: This uses the DFS traversal from L1 as its core subroutine

timeComplexity.note: because in worst case (complete graph), each DFS explores O(n) nodes and we do this n times

spaceComplexity.note: for recursion stack and visited sets

commonMistakes: Not resetting visited set for each starting node (leading to false positives)
explanation.codePattern: def canFinish(n, prerequisites): adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for a,b in prerequisites: adj[b].append(a); def dfs(node, visited): if node in visited: return True; visited.add(node); for neighbor in adj[node]: if dfs(neighbor, visited.copy()): return True; return False; for i in range(n): if dfs(i, set()): return False; return True

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[]. Step 2: Start DFS from node 0: visited={0}, check neighbor 1, DFS from 1: visited={0,1}, no neighbors, return false. No cycle found from node 0. Step 3: Start DFS from node 1: visited={1}, no neighbors, return false. No cycle found from node 1. Step 4: All nodes processed without cycle, return true.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0]. Step 2: Start DFS from node 0: visited={0}, check neighbor 1, DFS from 1: visited={0,1}, check neighbor 0, node 0 is in visited, cycle detected, return true. Step 3: Since DFS from node 0 found cycle, return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal from a Single Starting Node
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a given starting node to detect if it can reach itself through any path.

explanation.prose: This is the most important helper function: a DFS traversal that starts from a specific node and explores all reachable nodes to check if the starting node can be reached again, indicating a cycle. The intuition is that in a directed graph, if starting from node A we can follow edges and eventually return to A, we have a cycle involving A. The algorithm: 1) Input: current node, visited set (nodes in current path). 2) If current node is already in visited set, return true (cycle detected). 3) Add current node to visited set. 4) For each neighbor of current node: a) Recursively call DFS on neighbor with visited set. b) If returns true, propagate true upward. 5) Return false if no cycle found. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) in worst case (exploring all nodes). Connection to L2: This implements the cycle detection logic from L2.

connectsTo.reason: This function is called n times in L0 to check all possible starting points

spaceComplexity.note: for recursion stack and visited set

commonMistakes: Not passing visited set correctly (should be copied to avoid mutation issues)
infinite recursion if graph has self-loops
explanation.codePattern: def dfs(node, visited): if node in visited: return True; visited.add(node); for neighbor in adj[node]: if dfs(neighbor, visited.copy()): return True; return False

Step 1: dfs(0, {}): visited={0}. Step 2: Check neighbor 1: dfs(1, {0}): visited={0,1}, check neighbor 2: dfs(2, {0,1}): visited={0,1,2}, no neighbors, return false. Step 3: false propagates up, final return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection via Path Revisiting
ladders.desc: The central technique of checking if a node appears twice in the same traversal path.

explanation.prose: This is the core insight: a cycle exists in a directed graph if and only if during a traversal, we encounter a node that's already in the current path. The intuition is simple: if we're walking along a path and see the same node twice, we've necessarily looped back, creating a cycle. This works because in DFS, we maintain the current path as a set of visited nodes. When we move to a neighbor, we check if it's already in this path. If yes, cycle detected. If not, we add it and continue. The algorithm: 1) Maintain a set 'path' representing nodes in current traversal. 2) When visiting a node: a) Check if node in path. b) If yes, return cycle detected. c) If no, add to path. 3) Recurse on neighbors. 4) Remove node from path when backtracking (or use immutable copies). Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per check. Connection to L3: This relies on graph traversal from L3.

connectsTo.reason: This logic is embedded in the DFS function

commonMistakes: Confusing cycle detection with general visited nodes (need separate tracking for current path vs all visited)
explanation.codePattern: if node in path_set: return True; path_set.add(node); for neighbor in neighbors: if dfs(neighbor, path_set): return True; return False

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if node 0 is in path set {0,1,2}. Step 2: It is present. Step 3: Return true immediately indicating cycle.
Step 1: Check if node 2 is in path set {0,1}. Step 2: It is not present. Step 3: Add 2 to path set making it {0,1,2}. Step 4: Continue traversal.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with DFS
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of traversing a graph using Depth-First Search.

explanation.prose: This is the building block: traversing a graph using DFS to visit all reachable nodes from a starting point. The intuition is that DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, making it natural for path-based problems. The algorithm: 1) Start at a node. 2) Mark it as visited (to avoid reprocessing). 3) For each unvisited neighbor: recursively apply DFS. 4) Base case: no unvisited neighbors. For directed graphs, we follow outgoing edges. The recursive nature makes it elegant for cycle detection. Connection to L4: This uses adjacency list access from L4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Graph Neighbors via Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of retrieving all neighbors of a node from adjacency list representation.

explanation.prose: This is a basic operation: given a graph represented as an adjacency list, retrieve all nodes that are directly reachable from a given node. The intuition is that an adjacency list is an array of lists where index i contains all nodes that have an edge from i. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs and allows O(1) access to neighbor list. The algorithm: 1) Have adjacency list 'adj' where adj[i] is list of neighbors of node i. 2) To get neighbors of node x: return adj[x]. For directed graphs, these are outgoing edges. Connection to L5: This builds on array/list indexing from L5.

For arrays/lists in Python-style: 1) Access element at index i: list[i]. 2) Iterate through all elements: for item in list: .. 3) Iterate with indices: for i in range(len(list)): .

approaches.name: DFS with State Tracking O(n)
approaches.summary: Use DFS with three states (unvisited, visiting, visited) to detect cycles efficiently in a single pass. Mark nodes as visiting during recursion and visited after completion to avoid redundant traversals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Course Schedule Feasibility with DFS State Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution to detect cycles in course prerequisites using DFS with three states.

explanation.prose: This problem determines if all courses can be taken given prerequisites, which is equivalent to detecting cycles in a directed graph. The DFS with state tracking approach efficiently solves this in O(n) time and space. Intuition: We model courses as nodes and prerequisites as directed edges. A cycle means impossible schedule. We use three states: UNVISITED (0), VISITING (1), VISITED (2). During DFS, marking nodes as VISITING while exploring their descendants helps detect back edges (edges to nodes currently in recursion stack), indicating cycles. Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency list from prerequisites. 2) Initialize state array with UNVISITED for all nodes. 3) For each UNVISITED node, run DFS. In DFS: mark node VISITING, recursively visit neighbors; if neighbor is VISITING, cycle detected; if DFS returns false, propagate false; after exploring all neighbors, mark VISITED and return true. 4) If all DFS calls return true, schedule is feasible. Time: O(V+E) where V=nodes, E=edges, each node/edge processed once. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list and recursion stack.

connectsTo.reason: This integrates the DFS helper with graph building and iteration

commonMistakes: Not resetting state after cycle detection
explanation.codePattern: def canFinish(numCourses, prerequisites): adj = [[] for _ in range(numCourses)]; for a,b in prerequisites: adj[b].append(a); state = [0]*numCourses; def dfs(node): if state[node]==1: return False; if state[node]==2: return True; state[node]=1; for nei in adj[node]: if not dfs(nei): return False; state[node]=2; return True; for i in range(numCourses): if state[i]==0: if not dfs(i): return False; return True

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[]. Step 2: Initialize state: [0,0]. Step 3: Start DFS from node 0 (UNVISITED). Mark state[0]=VISITING(1). Explore neighbor 1: state[1]=UNVISITED(0), run DFS on 1. Mark state[1]=VISITING(1). No neighbors, mark state[1]=VISITED(2), return true. Back to node 0: all neighbors processed, mark state[0]=VISITED(2), return true. Step 4: All nodes visited, return true.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0]. Step 2: Initialize state: [0,0]. Step 3: Start DFS from node 0. Mark state[0]=VISITING(1). Explore neighbor 1: state[1]=UNVISITED(0), run DFS on 1. Mark state[1]=VISITING(1). Explore neighbor 0: state[0]=VISITING(1) → cycle detected, return false. Propagate false back to node 0, return false. Step 4: Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Helper with State Tracking
ladders.desc: The recursive DFS function that detects cycles using three states.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine performs DFS on a single node to detect cycles in its reachable subgraph. Intuition: By tracking states, we distinguish between nodes being actively explored (VISITING) and those fully processed (VISITED). A cycle occurs if we encounter a VISITING node during recursion, indicating a back edge. Step-by-step: Input: node index, adjacency list, state array. 1) If state[node] is VISITING, return false (cycle). 2) If state[node] is VISITED, return true (already processed). 3) Mark state[node] as VISITING. 4) For each neighbor in adjacency list: recursively call DFS; if returns false, propagate false. 5) After all neighbors processed, mark state[node] as VISITED and return true. Time: O(V+E) for the subgraph rooted at node, but overall O(V+E) across all calls. Space: O(V) for recursion stack in worst case.

connectsTo.reason: This function is called repeatedly in the main loop

commonMistakes: Forgetting to check VISITED state early (optimization)
or not propagating cycle detection correctly
explanation.codePattern: def dfs(node, adj, state): if state[node]==1: return False; if state[node]==2: return True; state[node]=1; for nei in adj[node]: if not dfs(nei, adj, state): return False; state[node]=2; return True

examples.trace: Step 1: state[0]=UNVISITED(0). Step 2: Mark state[0]=VISITING(1). Step 3: Explore neighbor 1: call dfs(1). state[1]=UNVISITED(0), mark VISITING(1). Explore neighbor 2: call dfs(2). state[2]=UNVISITED(0), mark VISITING(1). No neighbors, mark state[2]=VISITED(2), return true. Back to node 1: all neighbors processed, mark state[1]=VISITED(2), return true. Back to node 0: all neighbors processed, mark state[0]=VISITED(2), return true.

Step 1: state[0]=UNVISITED(0). Step 2: Mark state[0]=VISITING(1). Step 3: Explore neighbor 1: call dfs(1). state[1]=UNVISITED(0), mark VISITING(1). Explore neighbor 0: state[0]=VISITING(1) → cycle detected, return false. Propagate false back to node 0, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Three-State Cycle Detection in Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Using UNVISITED, VISITING, VISITED states to identify cycles during DFS.

explanation.prose: This core technique detects cycles in directed graphs by augmenting DFS with state tracking. Intuition: In standard DFS, we track visited nodes to avoid reprocessing, but that alone cannot distinguish between nodes in the current recursion path (potential cycle) and those fully processed. Three states solve this: UNVISITED (not processed), VISITING (currently in recursion stack), VISITED (fully processed). During DFS, when we encounter a neighbor, if it's VISITING, we've found a back edge → cycle; if VISITED, skip (already safe); if UNVISITED, recurse. This works because a cycle in a directed graph must involve a back edge to an ancestor in the DFS tree. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize all nodes as UNVISITED. 2) In DFS: mark node VISITING, process neighbors as above, after recursion mark VISITED. Time: O(V+E) as each node/edge processed once. Space: O(V) for state array.

connectsTo.reason: This logic is implemented within the DFS helper

commonMistakes: or not resetting state after cycle detection
explanation.codePattern: state = [0]*n; def dfs(u): if state[u]==1: return False; if state[u]==2: return True; state[u]=1; for v in adj[u]: if not dfs(v): return False; state[u]=2; return True

examples.input: Graph: 0→1→2→3, state tracking for DFS from 0
examples.trace: Start: state=[0,0,0,0]. DFS(0): mark state[0]=1, go to neighbor 1. DFS(1): mark state[1]=1, go to neighbor 2. DFS(2): mark state[2]=1, go to neighbor 3. DFS(3): mark state[3]=1, no neighbors, mark state[3]=2, return true. Back to 2: mark state[2]=2, return true. Back to 1: mark state[1]=2, return true. Back to 0: mark state[0]=2, return true. All states become 2, no VISITING encounters.

examples.input: Graph: 0→1→2→0 (cycle), state tracking for DFS from 0
examples.trace: Start: state=[0,0,0]. DFS(0): mark state[0]=1, go to neighbor 1. DFS(1): mark state[1]=1, go to neighbor 2. DFS(2): mark state[2]=1, go to neighbor 0. DFS(0): state[0]=1 (VISITING) → cycle detected, return false. Propagate back.

ladders.title: Building Block — Depth-First Search (DFS) on Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Traversing a directed graph using DFS to explore all reachable nodes.

explanation.prose: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. Intuition: Starting from a node, we recursively visit its neighbors, using a visited set to avoid revisiting nodes, ensuring each node is processed once. For directed graphs, edges have direction, so traversal follows outgoing edges only. Step-by-step: 1) Represent graph as adjacency list. 2) Initialize visited set. 3) Define recursive function dfs(node): mark visited, for each neighbor not visited, call dfs(neighbor). 4) Call dfs from starting nodes. This explores connected components. Time: O(V+E) where V=nodes, E=edges, as each node and edge is processed once. Space: O(V) for visited set and recursion stack.

connectsTo.reason: DFS forms the base; state tracking adds cycle detection

commonMistakes: infinite recursion in cycles without visited set
explanation.codePattern: def dfs(node, adj, visited): visited.add(node); for nei in adj[node]: if nei not in visited: dfs(nei, adj, visited)

examples.trace: Step 1: visited={}. Step 2: dfs(0): visited={0}, neighbor 1 not visited. Step 3: dfs(1): visited={0,1}, neighbor 2 not visited. Step 4: dfs(2): visited={0,1,2}, no neighbors. Backtrack to finish.
Step 1: visited={}. Step 2: dfs(0): visited={0}, neighbors 1 and 2. Step 3: dfs(1): visited={0,1}, no neighbors. Step 4: Back to 0, dfs(2): visited={0,1,2}, no neighbors.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Building an Adjacency List from Edge List
ladders.desc: Converting a list of directed edges into an adjacency list representation.

explanation.prose: An adjacency list is a common graph representation where each node has a list of its outgoing neighbors. Intuition: Given edges as pairs (u,v) meaning u depends on v or v→u, we build a list where index i contains nodes reachable from i. This efficiently supports graph traversal. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize empty list of size n (number of nodes). 2) For each edge (a,b) in prerequisites (meaning a depends on b, so edge from b to a): append a to adj[b]. 3) Result is a list of lists. Time: O(E) to process each edge once. Space: O(V+E) for the adjacency list.

connectsTo.reason: This provides the graph structure for DFS

explanation.codePattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for a,b in edges: adj[b]

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process edge [1,0]: append 1 to adj[0] → adj=[[1],[],[]]. Step 3: Process edge [2,0]: append 2 to adj[0] → adj=[[1,2],[],[]]. Final: adj[0]=[1,2], adj[1]=[], adj[2]=[].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Cycles
ladders.desc: Understanding directed graphs and what constitutes a cycle.

explanation.prose: A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arrows from one node to another). Intuition: Edges represent relationships with direction, like prerequisites where course A must come before course B. A cycle in a directed graph is a path that starts and ends at the same node, following the direction of edges. For example, if A→B→C→A, it's a cycle. Cycles make sequences impossible because they create circular dependencies. Step-by-step to identify cycles: 1) Visualize graph with nodes and arrows. 2) Look for paths that loop back. 3) If no cycle exists, graph is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). Time: Basic understanding, no complexity. Space: Conceptual.

connectsTo.reason: This foundation motivates why we build graphs and detect cycles

commonMistakes: or missing cycles in complex paths

examples.trace: Nodes: 0,1,2. Edges: 0 to 1, 1 to 2. Paths: 0→1, 1→2, 0→1→2. No path returns to start, so no cycle.
Nodes: 0,1,2. Edges: 0 to 1, 1 to 2, 2 to 0. Path: 0→1→2→0 returns to 0, forming a cycle.

approaches.name: Kahn's Topological Sort O(n)
approaches.summary: Use BFS with an indegree array to iteratively remove nodes with zero indegree. If all nodes are processed, no cycle exists; otherwise, a cycle is detected. This is iterative and queue-based.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Course Schedule Feasibility with Kahn's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to detect cycles in a directed graph representing course prerequisites using Kahn's topological sort.

explanation.prose: This problem models courses as nodes and prerequisites as directed edges (course A → course B means A must be taken before B). A cycle indicates impossible prerequisites. Kahn's algorithm detects cycles by processing nodes with zero indegree (no prerequisites) iteratively. The intuition: if there's no cycle, we can remove all nodes by starting with those having no dependencies. If nodes remain unprocessed, they form a cycle. Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency list and indegree array from input. 2) Initialize queue with nodes having indegree 0. 3) While queue not empty: dequeue node, add to result, decrement indegree of neighbors; if neighbor's indegree becomes 0, enqueue it. 4) After processing, if result size equals total nodes, no cycle; else cycle exists. Time: O(V+E) where V is nodes (courses), E is edges (prerequisites). Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list and indegree.

connectsTo.reason: This integrates the indegree processing subroutine

commonMistakes: forgetting to build adjacency list correctly

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency: {0: [1]}, indegree: [0,1]. Step 2: Queue initialized with node 0 (indegree 0). Step 3: Process node 0: result = [0], decrement indegree of neighbor 1 → indegree: [0,0], enqueue node 1. Process node 1: result = [0,1]. Step 4: Result size = 2 equals numCourses → no cycle → true.
Step 1: Adjacency: {0: [1], 1: [0]}, indegree: [1,1]. Step 2: No nodes with indegree 0 → queue empty. Step 3: No processing. Step 4: Result size = 0 ≠ 2 → cycle detected → false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Process Nodes with Zero Indegree Using BFS
ladders.desc: Implement the BFS loop that repeatedly removes nodes with no incoming edges and updates indegrees of neighbors.

explanation.prose: This subroutine is the core of Kahn's algorithm, performing the topological sorting via BFS. Intuition: By processing nodes with zero indegree, we simulate taking courses with no prerequisites, freeing up their dependent courses. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize queue with all nodes having indegree 0. 2) While queue not empty: dequeue node, add to result list, for each neighbor in adjacency list, decrement its indegree; if indegree becomes 0, enqueue neighbor. 3) Return result list. Time: O(V+E) as each node and edge processed once. Space: O(V) for queue and result.

connectsTo.reason: This subroutine is called after building graph structures in the full problem

commonMistakes: Not using a queue (using stack changes order)
forgetting to check indegree before enqueueing
or infinite loops if indegree not properly decremented
explanation.codePattern: queue = deque([i for i in range(n) if indegree[i] == 0]), while queue: node = queue

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue init with node 0 (indegree 0). Step 2: Process node 0: result = [0], decrement indegree of neighbors 1 and 2 → indegree: [0,0,0,2], enqueue 1 and 2. Process node 1: result = [0,1], decrement indegree of neighbor 3 → indegree: [0,0,0,1]. Process node 2: result = [0,1,2], decrement indegree of neighbor 3 → indegree: [0,0,0,0], enqueue 3. Process node 3: result = [0,1,2,3]. Queue empty → return [0,1,2,3].
Step 1: No nodes with indegree 0 → queue empty. Step 2: No processing → result = [].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Indegree Tracking for Dependency Resolution
ladders.desc: Maintain an indegree array to count incoming edges for each node, enabling detection of nodes ready for processing.

explanation.prose: The indegree array is central to Kahn's algorithm, representing how many prerequisites each course has. Intuition: Nodes with indegree 0 have no dependencies and can be processed first, reducing indegrees of their dependents. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize indegree array of size n with zeros. 2) For each edge (u,v) in prerequisites, increment indegree[v] by 1 (since u must be taken before v). 3) Use this array to find start nodes and update during BFS. Time: O(E) to build, O(1) per update. Space: O(V).

connectsTo.reason: This array is input to the BFS subroutine

commonMistakes: Incorrectly incrementing indegree for source instead of target
explanation.codePattern: indegree = [0]*n, for u,v in prerequisites: indegree[v] += 1

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize indegree = [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process [1,0]: indegree[1]++ → [0,1,0,0]. Process [2,0]: indegree[2]++ → [0,1,1,0]. Process [3,1]: indegree[3]++ → [0,1,1,1]. Process [3,2]: indegree[3]++ → [0,1,1,2]. Final indegree: [0,1,1,2].
Step 1: indegree = [0,0,0]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: indegree[1]++ → [0,1,0]. Process [1,2]: indegree[2]++ → [0,1,1]. Process [2,0]: indegree[0]++ → [1,1,1]. Final indegree: [1,1,1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Construct an adjacency list from a list of edges to represent a directed graph efficiently.

explanation.prose: An adjacency list is a data structure where each node has a list of its outgoing neighbors, ideal for sparse graphs like course prerequisites. Intuition: It allows quick access to all courses that depend on a given course. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an empty list of lists of size n. 2) For each edge (u,v) in prerequisites, append v to the list at index u (since u points to v). 3) This represents directed edges from prerequisite to course. Time: O(E) to build. Space: O(V+E).

connectsTo.reason: The adjacency list is used with indegree array in BFS

explanation.codePattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)], for u,v in prerequisites: adj[u]

examples.trace: Step 1: adj = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: append 1 to adj[0] → [[1],[],[]]. Process [1,2]: append 2 to adj[1] → [[1],[2],[]]. Process [2,0]: append 0 to adj[2] → [[1],[2],[0]]. Final adj: [[1],[2],[0]].
